title: Personalized automatic management of tracheal cuff pressure and subglottic secretions drainage to prevent pneumonia in critically ill intubated patients. The MICROINHALO multicenter randomized controlled trial
one_line_summary: The MICROINHALO trial found that an automatic ETT cuff pressure and subglottic secretion drainage system **significantly** reduced clinically and microbiologically confirmed VAP, despite no difference in early tracheal colonization, ICU stay, or **mortality**.
key_pearls: Manual ETT cuff pressure checks every 8 hours are often insufficient, leading to high rates of under-inflation (<**20 cmH2O**) and increased microaspiration risk.
Automated cuff pressure management systems maintain Pcuff within the 20-**30 cmH2O** range more effectively (10.2% vs. 24.5% out-of-range, P < 0.001).
Automated subglottic secretion drainage (e.g., AnapnoGuard) clears **significantly** more secretions (**25 mL**/day vs. **10.5 mL**/day) compared to manual methods.
The AnapnoGuard system **significantly** reduced clinically diagnosed VAP (12.6% vs. 24.4%, P = 0.016) and microbiologically confirmed VAP (10.2% vs. 19.5%, P = 0.039).
Patients requiring prolonged mechanical ventilation (>48-72 hours) may benefit from automated Pcuff and SSD systems to reduce VAP.
VAP prevention with these devices reduces infectious complications and antibiotic exposure but does not automatically improve ventilator-free days, ICU length of stay, or **mortality**.
population: Adult patients (> 18 years) requiring invasive mechanical ventilation (IMV) with an expected duration > 48 hours.

sections.heading: Trial Design, Population, and Baseline Characteristics
sections.content: This was a cluster-randomized, international, open-label, controlled trial conducted across 10 ICUs in Italy and Israel between June 2022 and April 2024. Randomization was performed in predetermined clusters of 9 consecutive patients, stratified by center, to avoid practical difficulties of individual emergency randomization. Participants were adult patients (> 18 years) requiring invasive mechanical ventilation (IMV) with an expected duration > 48 hours. Exclusion criteria included IMV in the previous 14 days, contraindications to enteral feeding, enrollment in conflicting trials, clinical evidence of aspiration prior to intubation, and pregnancy. Out of 270 randomized patients, 250 were included in the modified intention-to-treat analysis (127 in the Automatic Management [AM] group, 123 in the Manual Management [MM] group). Baseline demographics were well-balanced between groups, with a median age of 66 years in AM and 65 years in MM, and a median Charlson Comorbidity Index of 5 in both groups. Notably, 50.8% of the overall cohort was intubated with a diagnosis of suspected pneumonia (53.5% in AM vs. 47.9% in MM), and approximately 76% were already receiving antibiotics at ICU admission.

sections.heading: Interventions and Technical Specifications
sections.content: In the Automatic Management (AM) group, patients were intubated with the AnapnoGuard ETT (polyvinylchloride tube, ellipsoidal shape, thin-wall polyurethane cuff) featuring dual suction lines and an extra venting line. This was connected to the AnapnoGuard 100 control unit, which intermittently measured CO2 levels in the subglottic space to detect air leaks, automatically adjusted Pcuff to the lowest pressure required to seal the trachea, and evacuated subglottic secretions automatically by simultaneously rinsing/venting the space from one port while performing intermittent suctioning through dedicated ports. In the Manual Management (MM) group, patients were intubated with the TaperGuard Evac ETT (polyurethane conic cuff, dorsal suction lumen). Pcuff was manually checked and maintained between 20-**30 cmH2O** using a portable manometer at least every 8 hours, and subglottic secretions were manually drained using a **10 mL** syringe with an intended frequency of once per hour.

sections.heading: Primary Endpoint: Tracheal Colonization
sections.content: The primary endpoint was bacterial tracheal colonization, defined as > 1000 CFU/mL from quantitative tracheal aspirate (TA) on Day 3 after randomization, in the absence of clinical/radiological signs of VAP. Results showed that tracheal colonization on Day 3 occurred in 37% (47/127) of the AM group compared to 41.5% (51/123) of the MM group (**absolute** difference -4% [95% CI -16 to 8], P = 0.52), indicating no **significant** difference. Subgroup and sensitivity analyses also did not show superiority: new isolated bacteria (> 1000 CFU/mL) on Day 3 were 26% in AM vs. 28.5% in MM (P = 0.67), and Day 3 tracheal colonization at any bacterial count was 49.6% in AM vs. 51.2% in MM (P = 0.80). A post-hoc Bayesian sensitivity analysis did not reach the threshold for superiority (posterior probability of 72-79%). Microbiological isolates on Day 3 were predominantly Gram-negative (65.4%) compared to Gram-positive (34.6%), including 47 multidrug-resistant (MDR) strains.

sections.heading: Secondary Endpoints: VAP, Cuff Pressure, and Secretion Drainage
sections.content: Regarding Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia (VAP), clinically diagnosed VAP was **significantly** lower in the AM group (12.6% [16/127]) than in the MM group (24.4% [30/123]) (OR 0.45 [95% CI 0.23-0.87], P = 0.016). Microbiologically confirmed VAP was also **significantly** lower in the AM group (10.2% [13/127]) than in the MM group (19.5% [24/123]) (OR 0.47 [95% CI 0.23-0.97], P = 0.039). The VAP rate per 1000 MV days was 12.5 vs. 24.6 for clinically diagnosed VAP and 10.2 vs. 19.7 for microbiologically confirmed VAP in the AM and MM groups, respectively. The median time to VAP event was 6 days in the MM group and 7 days in the AM group. For Cuff Pressure (Pcuff) Control, the percentage of Pcuff measurements outside the 20-**30 cmH2O** safety range was 10.2% in the AM group vs. 24.5% in the MM group (P < 0.001). In the AM group, all 264 out-of-range measures were > **30 cmH2O**, automatically driven to seal the trachea during detected leaks. In the MM group, out of 541 out-of-range measures, 377 were under-inflated (< **20 cmH2O**), with 240 measurements falling below **16 cmH2O**, posing a high risk for microaspiration. Mean per-patient Pcuff was higher in the AM group (27.1 [IQR 26.4-28.3] cmH2O) than in the MM group (24.7 [IQR 22.1-27.1] cmH2O, P < 0.001). Finally, for Subglottic Secretion Drainage (SSD) Volume, the median daily SSD volume was **significantly** higher in the AM group: 25 [IQR 8-41] mL vs. 10.5 [IQR 6-17] mL in the MM group (P < 0.001). The median total SSD volume per patient was 126 [IQR 45-249] mL in the AM group vs. 49 [IQR 30-119.5] mL in the MM group (P < 0.001).

sections.heading: Other Clinical Outcomes
sections.content: There were no statistically **significant** differences between the AM and MM groups in several other clinical outcomes. These included post-extubation stridor (2.4% vs. 1.6%, P = 1.00), tracheostomy rate (16.5% vs. 13.0%, P = 0.48), 28-day mechanical ventilation-free days (21 vs. 20 days, P = 0.89), 28-day antibiotic-free days (19 vs. 19 days, P = 0.65), 28-day ICU-free days (17 vs. 18 days, P = 0.77), hospital length of stay (23 vs. 21 days, P = 0.39), 28-day **mortality** (41.7% vs. 43.9%, P = 0.70), 90-day **mortality** (46.5% vs. 49.6%, P = 0.53), and in-ICU **mortality** (38.6% vs. 42.3%, P = 0.78).

strengths_limitations: Strengths include its multicenter, international, randomized design with an active control group and strict VAP diagnostic criteria. The study also provided objective physiological measurements of cuff pressure and secretion volumes. Limitations include the open-label design, which could introduce diagnostic bias for clinical VAP, and a high baseline colonization rate that may have diluted the primary endpoint results. Additionally, the primary endpoint of Day 3 tracheal colonization might be a surrogate that doesn't perfectly reflect later VAP development, and diagnostic heterogeneity for VAP could be a concern.
